title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the balloon. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Lighter Than Air: The Age of the Balloon
content.text: The dream of flight is as old as humanity, but for thousands of years, it remained a fantasy of myth and legend. The first real breakthrough occurred not with wings, but with a simple observation about smoke. In the late 18th century, two French brothers, Joseph and Étienne Montgolfier, noticed that heated air trapped inside a paper bag caused it to rise toward the ceiling. ||(41)|| This first public demonstration in 1783 was a sensation. A massive balloon made of paper and silk, decorated with golden suns and zodiac signs, rose 2,000 meters above the town of Annonay. To test the safety of the air, the brothers sent up a sheep, a duck, and a rooster, all of whom returned to Earth unharmed. ||(42)|| This 'Montgolfière' era was quickly challenged by a different scientific approach. Jacques Charles, a physicist, realized that hydrogen—a gas recently discovered to be much lighter than air—would provide a more consistent and powerful lift. His 'Charlière' balloon was made of rubberized silk to prevent the tiny gas molecules from escaping. ||(43)|| The rivalry between 'fire' and 'gas' balloons captivated the public, but the fundamental problem of flight remained: the balloon was at the mercy of the wind. A pilot could go up or down, but they had no way to steer. This led to a century of strange experiments with oars, sails, and even teams of trained eagles. ||(44)|| The solution finally arrived with the invention of the internal combustion engine. By attaching a motor and a propeller to an elongated, cigar-shaped balloon, inventors like Alberto Santos-Dumont created the 'airship' or dirigible. In 1901, he famously flew around the Eiffel Tower, proving that man could finally navigate the skies with precision. ||(45)|| This led to the golden age of the zeppelin, massive silver giants that crossed the Atlantic in luxury. However, the era of the hydrogen-filled airship came to a tragic end in 1937 with the Hindenburg disaster. The sight of the pride of the German fleet bursting into flames in seconds effectively ended public confidence in lighter-than-air transport. ||(46)|| Today, the hot air balloon has returned as a symbol of leisure and adventure. Using modern nylon fabrics and powerful propane burners, enthusiasts fly for the sheer joy of the view. While we now rely on jet engines for travel, the quiet, drifting flight of the balloon reminds us of that first miraculous moment when we finally broke our bond with the Earth.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] this simple discovery [/41] led them to build a larger-scale version using fabric and paper. They believed they had discovered a new kind of 'gas' produced by the fire, which they called 'Montgolfier gas,' unaware that it was simply the expansion of heated air.
paragraphs.B: Following [42] this successful animal flight [/42], the King of France gave permission for the first human ascent. On November 21, 1783, Pilâtre de Rozier and the Marquis d'Arlandes became the first humans to achieve sustained flight, drifting over the rooftops of Paris for 25 minutes.
paragraphs.C: This [43] use of hydrogen [/43] allowed for much longer and higher flights. Unlike the Montgolfier balloon, which required a constant, dangerous fire on board, the gas balloon was silent and cool, making it the preferred choice for early scientific observations of the atmosphere.
paragraphs.D: Despite [44] these creative efforts [/44], the balloon remained a 'noble plaything.' It was used for fairs and exhibitions, but its lack of maneuverability meant it could not be used for serious transport or military reconnaissance until a reliable power source was found.
paragraphs.E: This [45] newfound control [/45] transformed the balloon from a curiosity into a strategic asset. During the early 20th century, airships were used for everything from long-distance mail delivery to spotting submarines during the First World War.
paragraphs.F: This [46] decline in popularity [/46] was hastened by the rapid advancement of the airplane. Wings were faster, cheaper to maintain, and far less susceptible to the wind, making the majestic but slow-moving airship an evolutionary dead-end in the history of aviation.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern balloonists [/47] participate in massive festivals, where hundreds of colorful balloons fill the sky at once. These events celebrate the history of the craft while using GPS and radio technology to ensure the safety of the pilots and passengers.

questions.explanation: A refers to the 'simple discovery' of heated air and the brothers' belief in 'Montgolfier gas' before their public demonstration.
B follows the 'animal flight' (sheep, duck, rooster) and introduces the first 'human ascent' by Rozier and d'Arlandes.
C describes the benefits of 'hydrogen' over heated air, following the introduction of Jacques Charles’s 'Charlière' balloon.
D refers to the 'creative efforts' (oars, sails) mentioned before the gap and explains why the balloon remained a 'plaything' until motors were invented.
E links the 'newfound control' of the dirigible (Santos-Dumont) to its use as a 'strategic asset' in mail and war.
F explains the 'decline in popularity' of airships following the Hindenburg disaster and the rise of the airplane.
